coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Ce doped NiO nanocomposite for high performance supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Enizi, Abdullah M.
Ubaidullah, Mohd
Kumar, Dinesh
coredata.dc:description: A cost effective novel carbon quantum dots/Ce-doped nickel oxide (CQDs/Ce-NiO) nanocomposite was synthesized via hydrothermal route. An exceptional BET surface area of 731 m2 g−1 and mesoporosity (∼17 nm) was estimated. In three-electrode system an excellent electrochemical behaviour was demonstrated by CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite with high specific capacitance of ∼2487 F g-1 by cyclic voltammograms at a scan rate of 5 mV s-1 and ∼2513 F g-1 by galvanostatic charge-discharge at a current density of 0.5 A g-1 in 6M KOH electrolyte. This may be owing to elegant redox activity of Ce3+/Ce4+ ions and high conductivity of CQDs. The capacitance retention was found ∼97 % after 2000 GCD cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal synthesis
Carbon quantum dots
Metal oxide
Nanocomposite
Electrochemical analysis
Energy storage and conversion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FE-SEM (b) TEM (c) SAED (d) particle size distribution histogram (e) XRD and (f) FTIR of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm (b) BJH pore size distribution and (c) multipoint BET analysis of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.
(a) XPS survey (b) Ni 2p high resolution spectrum (c) C1s high resolution and (d) O1s high resolution spectrum of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.

(a) CV plots (b) specific capacitance versus scan rates plot (c) GCD plots (d) specific capacitance versus current density plot (e) GCD cyclic stability and (f) Nyquist plot of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.
Specific capacitance comparison of previously reported materials and CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Ce doped NiO nanocomposite for high performance supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, Riyadh, 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451, Saudi

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inorganic and Nanochemistry, School of Chemical Sciences, Central University of Gujarat, Gandhinagar, 382030, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Inorganic and Nanochemistry, School of Chemical Sciences, Central University of Gujarat, Gandhinagar, 382030, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A cost effective novel carbon quantum dots/Ce-doped nickel oxide (CQDs/Ce-NiO) nanocomposite was synthesized via hydrothermal route. An exceptional BET surface area of 731 m2 g−1 and mesoporosity (∼17 nm) was estimated. In three-electrode system an excellent electrochemical behaviour was demonstrated by CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite with high specific capacitance of ∼2487 F g-1 by cyclic voltammograms at a scan rate of 5 mV s-1 and ∼2513 F g-1 by galvanostatic charge-discharge at a current density of 0.5 A g-1 in 6M KOH electrolyte. This may be owing to elegant redox activity of Ce3+/Ce4+ ions and high conductivity of CQDs. The capacitance retention was found ∼97 % after 2000 GCD cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous surging demand of energy and depletion of fossil fuels leads to the quest of smart energy materials, which can offer a hopeful solution/s to overcome the energy crisis. Supercapacitors (SCs) are the key solutions for several advanced applications for instance handy electronic devices, renewable power grids and hybrid electric vehicles. As per charge–discharge mechanisms two main types of SCs are as follows: (1) on the basis of charge separation at the electrode/ electrolyte interface termed as electrical double layer capacitors (EDLC) and (2) attributed to reversible redox reactions at the electrode named pseudo-capacitors (PCs) [1]. Owing to high theoretical capacitance NiO regarded the good PCs electrode material but poor cyclic stability restricts it for commercial use. In this report we proposed and fabricated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and cerium (Ce) doped NiO nanocomposite to overcome this problem, because of the high redox behaviour of Cerium (Ce3+ to Ce4+ vice versa), excellent conductivity and surface area of CQDs. Gawali et.al reported Ce:NiO/rGO in KOH electrolyte and found a highest Cs of 462 F g−1 was for 1 % Ce : NiO sample [2]. Anjali et.al reported Ce-doped NiO NPs layered on titanium foils and highest specific capacitance of 155 F g−1 in KOH [3]. Moreover, saranya et.al reported Ce doped NiO nanoflakes mesoporous 3D network and achieved 2444 F g-1 specific capacitance at 05 mV s-1 [4], while Xu et.al reported CQDs/NiO nanorods and achieved specific capacitance of 1858 F g-1 at a current density of 1 A g-1 in 6 M KOH [5]. As we figure out, this is the first report on CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.

Ce(NO3)3.6H2O (99.99 %), Ni(NO3)2.6H2O (99 %), HCL, KOH, Ammonia (BDH), NMP (N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone) purchased from BDH Chemicals Ltd, England and used as received. 1%-Ce doped NiO nanocomposite was prepared as per earlier reports [4].

CQDs were prepared via an elegant pyrolysis route [6] by taking 0.75 g of sucrose (C12H22O11, ≥99.5 Aldrich, USA) mixed in 30 mL deionized water at 27 °C with continuous stirring for ½ h. This was shifted to 100 mL stainless autoclave (Teflon) and treated at 180 °C for 5 h and cooled at 27 °C. This was collected and washed with ethanol and deionized water followed by vacuum drying at 60 °C and the obtained product is termed as carbon quantum dots CQDs.

In 50 mL of distilled water, 3 wt % of CQDs and 100 mg of Ce-NiO were dispersed with continuous stirring for 15 min, followed by 10 min ultrasonic treatment [6]. This sample was compiled by centrifugation and dried in vacuum at 40 °C for 12 h, further heated at 300 °C for 2 h in Argon at a heating and cooling rate 5 °C/minute. The obtained powder was referred as CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposites. FE-SEM (JEOL 7600 F), HR-TEM (FEI-Technai) for surface morphological features and shape/size respectively. Elemental analysis was carried out through CHN (Perkin Elmer, 2400) and ICP-OES (Varian VISTA). Phase purity (PXRD) and functional groups (FTIR) were detected via Bruker (D-8 diffractometer) and an FTIR (Bruker Tensor 27) comprehensive surface area and pore size was calculated by BET surface area instrument (Quantachrome; USA). Moreover, electrochemical behaviour of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite electrodes were investigated on CHI-electrochemical work station by examining CV, GCD, and EIS in 6 M KOH with three-electrode systems. The preparation method of the working electrode for electrochemical studies is as follows.

The working electrode of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite was as follows. Typically, 95 wt.% CQDs/NiO, 5 wt.% polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) as a binder were homogeneously mixed together to form a slurry, which was pasted on a area of 1 × 1 cm nickel form piece. The electrochemical behavior of the CQDs/NiO electrode was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with the help of a CHI-electrochemical work station.

FE-SEM micrograph (Fig. 1 a) illustrates uniformly distributed close packed spherical agglomerated particles. TEM micrograph showed clearly visible overlapped spherical particles (∼19 nm) exhibited in Fig. 1b. Selected area electron diffraction rings of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite in which three rings are more clear and rest are blur/faint, marked as (110), (200), (220), (311) and (222) planes shown in Fig. 1c, these diffraction patterns are corroborated well with XRD pattern. Particle size distribution histogram measured from the TEM micrograph show variation in the particle size. The TEM particles sizes were calculated in 8–32 nm range with an average diameter size of 19 nm demonstrated in Fig. 1d. XRD peaks indexed with JCPDS card no;78−0643, obtained at 36.89°, 42.96°, 62.56°, 75.12° and 79.24° 2θ can be ascribed face centered cubic planes of NiO i.e. (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222). A small peak at 2θ of 23.57° validates the presence of carbon content in the material. It was noted that XRD pattern of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposites depicting a small shift of 111 and 200 planes approaching to lower angle as equated to NiO (pure) this is because of the strain in lattice induced by the higher ionic radius (114 pm) of Ce3+ ions, on the substitution of smaller (69 pm) ions of Ni2+ [4].

Additionally, FTIR studies of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite exhibits low intense band at 572 cm−1 certified to MO vibrations [7], while the band at 1222 cm−1 belongs to CH stretching vibration [4]. The bands located at 1589 cm−1, 3429 belongs to bending and stretching of hydroxyl group [4]. The small band at 2553 cm−1 assigned to CO2 species absorbed from environment [8]. After final heat treatment the elemental investigation and ICP-OES showed Ni (63.21 %), Ce (0.85 %), C (13.42 %), H (0.03 %) and O (22.12 %) in which, adequate amount of carbon and cerium can enhance the electrochemical behaviour of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite. The detailed surface area analysis in liquid nitrogen at 77 K exhibited in Fig. 2 (a–c). N2 adsorption- desorption isotherm of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite matched with type IV isotherm of H3 hysteresis loop with high BET surface area of 731 m2 g−1 estimated in a P/P0 scale of 0.05−0.35 through multipoint BET equation as depicted in Fig. 2 (a). Moreover, the BJH pore size analysis confirmed that maximum pores are in 17 nm range, which established the mesoporous nature of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite as shown in Fig. 2 (b). The multipoint BET plot of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite shown in Fig. 2 (c).

XPS studies (Fig. 3 a–d) was performed to establish the binding energy and oxidation states of the elements present at the surface of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite. Moreover, this study shows the existence of Ce into the NiO sites. The survey spectra of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite recognized the existence of Ni, C, O and Ce only, which confirm the ultra-purity of nanocomposite as illustrated in Fig. 3a. The presence of Ce 3d signal confirmed the addition of Ce ions in Ni2+ lattice. The low intense peak of Ce 3d due to low concentration of dopant (Ce-1%). The deconvolution of Ni 2p high resolution spectrum exhibit two prime peaks at 875.59 eV and 858.50 eV attributed to the orbital splitting level of Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2, with the energy separation between the two peaks is about 17.09 eV which validates the oxidation state of Ni ion exists in 2+ valence state as depicted in Fig. 2b [4]. Fig. 3c showed the C1s high resolution spectrum can be fitted to three peaks indicated at 286.80, 285.86, and 284.22 eV and ascribed to O-C = O, C-O-C, and C-C or C = C respectively [9]. The graphitization of carbon content in the form of CQDs was established by the prominent peak marked at 284.62 eV (C-C/C = C) [10]. The substitutional doping of Ce3+ resulted in lattice distortion during the incorporation of Ce ion into NiO lattice site which was also established by XRD studies [4].The mesopores present in the materials can act as a reservoir for electrolyte ions which improve the electrochemical properties of the electrode material.

Comprehensive electrochemical analysis i.e. CV, GCD, capacitance retention and EIS of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite were recorded in 6 M KOH electrolyte with three-electrode system shown in Fig. 4 (a–d). A potential window from -0.4 to 0.6 V was scanned for CV and GCD analysis (Fig. 4a, c) with different scan rate (5 - 200 mV s−1/figure. 4a)/current density (0.5-1.0 A g−1/Fig. 4c) rates.

An excellent specific capacitance of ∼2487 F g−1 estimated by cyclic voltammogram at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, while specific capacitance of ∼2513 F g−1 calculated by galvanostatic charge-discharge at a current density of 0.5 A g-1 as shown in Fig. 4b,d. Table 1 . Illustrate the specific capacitance comparison of previously reported materials and CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.
Furthermore, the overall charge storage mechanism is as follows;

The charge storage process of NiO nanoflakes is given by the following equations,
(1) NiO + OH− → NiOOH + e−
(2) NiOOH + e− →NiO + OH-

Moreover, the charge storage mechanism here is not related to EDLC behavior but it is associated with the transition of Ce3+/Ce4+ ions during the redox reaction. The charge storage mechanism in Ce doped NiO electrodes is stated in the following Equation
(3) Ce:NiO + OH− → Ce:NiOOH + e-

Furthermore, specific capacitance retention rate was found 97 % after 200 GCD cycles shown in Fig. 4e. The electrochemical impedance measurements (EIS) were carried out at 5 mV from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz shown in Fig. 4f. The inset in Fig. 4 (f) showed equivalent cyclic circuit, obtained by simulating the Impedance data of the composite with a Z-View software. The net impedance is the sum of the solution resistance (Rs) and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the materials. The diameters of the semicircles in high frequency region yield the Rct at the electrolyte interface, which reflected the conductivity of the electrode. The plot shows very low resistance charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 1 Ω for CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite.

A low-cost novel CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite was prepared via simple hydrothermal method with an excellent BET surface area of 731 m2 g−1 and mesoporosity (∼17 nm). In three-electrode system an excellent electrochemical behaviour was demonstrated by CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite with high specific capacitance of ∼2487 F g-1 by CV at scan rate of 5 mV s-1 and ∼2513 F g-1 by CGD at a current density 0.5 A g-1 in 6 M KOH electrolyte. This may be due to high redox activity of Ce3+/ Ce4+ ions and high conductivity of CQDs. The capacitance retention was found ∼97 % after 2000 GCD cycles. Therefore, the good electrochemical response and excellent redox activity of CQDs/Ce-NiO nanocomposite makes it a potential candidate for energy storage devices.

Abdullah M. Al-Enizi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Mohd Ubaidullah: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Dinesh Kumar: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Data curation, Formal analysis, Resources, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to Deanship of Scientific Research at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through research group No (RG-1435-010).
